_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Pembina County, ND. The oldest county in North Dakota — fur trade records predate American sovereignty. County seat moved from Pembina to Cavalier in 1887. Record survival is complicated by the pre-territorial fur trade era and the county seat relocation.
display_name: Pembina County, North Dakota — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Pembina — original county seat at the mouth of the Pembina River
previous_courthouses.fate: The county seat moved to Cavalier in 1887 when railroad construction bypassed old Pembina and the population center shifted inland. Records were transferred to Cavalier.

records_destroyed: HBC and NWC trading records for the American side
record_losses.scope: significant for the pre-1867 period; minimal for the post-organization period
record_losses.description: Pembina's pre-territorial history creates a unique record gap. The fur trade post was established in 1797, and the Metis community existed for decades before any American civil authority. Hudson's Bay Company records for the area are held in Winnipeg. Catholic parish records from St. Joseph's Mission at Pembina (est. 1818) predate any civil record by nearly fifty years. When Pembina County was formally organized under Dakota Territory in 1867, the civil record begins — but the community was already old. The county seat relocation from Pembina to Cavalier in 1887 transferred records without known loss.
record_losses.narrative_impact: For ancestors in Pembina County before 1867, civil records do not exist. Catholic parish records, Hudson's Bay Company journals, and the records of the Red River Settlement (Manitoba) are the primary sources. After 1867, county records are good.
record_losses.source: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003. Archives of Manitoba, Winnipeg.

deeds.completeness: Good from county organization. Pre-1867 land tenure was informal — Metis families held land by community custom, not by recorded deed.
deeds.gaps: The entire pre-1867 era has no civil deed records. Some Metis land claims were formalized in the 1870s-1880s through homestead filings or patent applications, but many were not.
deeds.repository: Pembina County Recorder's Office, Cavalier; SHSND

wills_estates.gaps: Pre-territorial estates were settled informally. Few probate cases before 1880.
wills_estates.repository: Pembina County Clerk of Court; SHSND

marriages.completeness: Good from early 1870s. Catholic marriages at Pembina predate civil licensing by decades — the parish register at St. Joseph's records marriages from 1818.
marriages.gaps: Catholic and Metis marriages before 1872 appear only in church records.

court_minutes.gaps: No civil court records before 1867. Disputes in the fur trade era were resolved through HBC or community custom.
court_minutes.repository: Pembina County Clerk of Court; SHSND

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1910. Catholic parish records at Pembina are essential vital records for the pre-1893 era — and for the pre-1867 era, they are the only vital records.
vital_records.repository: ND Department of Health Vital Records; SHSND

substitute_sources.source_type: Catholic parish records — St. Joseph's Mission, Pembina
substitute_sources.description: St. Joseph's Mission at Pembina was established in 1818 by Father Severe Dumoulin. The parish maintained sacramental registers — baptisms, marriages, burials — in French and Latin. These records document the Metis community at Pembina for nearly fifty years before any civil authority existed.
substitute_sources.repository: Archdiocese of Fargo Archives; Diocese of St. Boniface Archives (Winnipeg); SHSND
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The single most important genealogical source for pre-territorial Pembina County. Baptismal records name parents, godparents, and sometimes grandparents. Marriage records include witnesses and parents. These records connect Pembina Metis families to the Red River Settlement and to Quebec.
substitute_sources.source: Wilkins, Robert P., and Wynona Huchette Wilkins. North Dakota: A Bicentennial History. New York: Norton, 1977.

substitute_sources.description: HBC post journals, account books, and correspondence for the Pembina area document trade, employment, and community life in the fur trade era. These records are held primarily in Winnipeg.
substitute_sources.repository: Archives of Manitoba, Winnipeg (HBC Archives); Library and Archives Canada
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies Metis and fur trade families by name, employment, and family connection. Account books list individuals by name with trade goods and credits. The records are in English and French.
substitute_sources.source: Podruchny, Carolyn. Making the Voyageur World. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 2006.

substitute_sources.description: A distinctive Icelandic settlement cluster along the Tongue River in western Pembina County maintained community records, church registers (Lutheran), and correspondence with Iceland. This was one of the few Icelandic settlement areas in the United States.
substitute_sources.repository: SHSND; University of Manitoba Icelandic Collection; individual families
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies Icelandic families by farm of origin in Iceland. Icelandic naming conventions (patronymics) require understanding the naming system to trace families accurately.
substitute_sources.source: Walters, Thorstina. Modern Sagas: The Story of the Icelanders in North America. Fargo: NDIRS, 1953.

substitute_sources.description: Homestead application files with detailed personal information.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for homestead-era settlers. For Metis families, homestead filings sometimes formalized land claims held by community custom for decades.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Homestead Records.

narrative_hooks: Pembina County's civil records begin in 1867 — but {ancestor_name}'s family may have been there for decades before any American clerk recorded a deed. The Catholic parish at Pembina was baptizing children and burying the dead fifty years before Dakota Territory organized the county.

If {ancestor_name} was Metis, the records that matter most are in Winnipeg, not Cavalier — the HBC post journals and Catholic sacramental registers that documented the fur trade community at Pembina long before the homesteaders arrived.

The county seat moved from old Pembina to Cavalier in 1887, following the railroad inland — and {ancestor_name}'s records traveled with it, from the river junction where the fur trade had built a world to the railroad town that replaced it.

For {ancestor_name}'s Icelandic family on the Tongue River, the naming system itself is a genealogical puzzle — patronymics meant that father, son, and daughter all carried different surnames, and tracing the family requires understanding that Jonsson's daughter was Jonsdottir, not Jonsson.
source_references: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003.
Podruchny, Carolyn. Making the Voyageur World: Travelers and Traders in the North American Fur Trade. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 2006.
Wilkins, Robert P., and Wynona Huchette Wilkins. North Dakota: A Bicentennial History. New York: Norton, 1977.
Walters, Thorstina. Modern Sagas: The Story of the Icelanders in North America. Fargo: North Dakota Institute for Regional Studies, 1953.
Archives of Manitoba. Hudson's Bay Company Archives. Winnipeg.
